title: Mathematics for AI
description: Downloaded Math AI JSON exams imported from Downloads.

exams.title: Week 1 Part 1 - Scalars, Vectors, and Matrices
exams.description: Weekly Lecture Exams - Core notation for scalars, vectors, matrices, shapes, indexing, and AI data representations. Source: W1-P1 - Scalars Vectors and Matrices.pdf.

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes a vector?
choices: an ordered list of numbers that can represent a point, direction, feature row, or parameter set
a rule that removes the need to check assumptions
a random label assigned to a formula
a plotting convention with no algebraic meaning
questions.explanation: A vector is an ordered list of numbers that can represent a point, direction, feature row, or parameter set.

questions.prompt: Which statements are true for Scalars, Vectors, and Matrices?
choices: Vectors have both entries and an order
Changing the order of vector entries never changes the vector
A matrix can be interpreted as a table of numbers
Every matrix product AB is valid
questions.explanation: The true statements are: Vectors have both entries and an order and A matrix can be interpreted as a table of numbers.

questions.prompt: Multiplying matrices without checking inner dimensions is a safe general strategy.
choices: True
False
questions.explanation: False. Verify that the number of columns in the left matrix equals the number of rows in the right matrix.

questions.prompt: A student is working on storing feature values for an AI model. Which technique is most directly useful?
choices: tracking dimensions before multiplying
ignoring dimensions and units
choosing a formula only because it is longer
removing all assumptions from the problem
questions.explanation: tracking dimensions before multiplying is useful to catch incompatible matrix and vector operations.

questions.prompt: What is the best interpretation of this formula: If x has shape n x 1 and A has shape m x n, then Ax has shape m x 1.
choices: Shape checks prevent invalid matrix-vector products before doing calculations.
It proves that every matrix operation is valid
It says all model parameters must be zero
It removes the need for data preprocessing
questions.explanation: Shape checks prevent invalid matrix-vector products before doing calculations.

questions.prompt: A solution has this problem: The inner dimensions do not match. What is the best correction?
choices: Verify that the number of columns in the left matrix equals the number of rows in the right matrix
Continue with the same calculation and ignore the warning
Switch every vector into a scalar
Assume the final answer is correct because the notation looks familiar
questions.explanation: Verify that the number of columns in the left matrix equals the number of rows in the right matrix

questions.prompt: Which ideas should you check when applying a vector in an AI or data problem?
choices: The shape of a matrix controls which products are valid
to catch incompatible matrix and vector operations
A scalar must always be written as a 1 x 1 matrix
Multiplying matrices without checking inner dimensions
questions.explanation: Useful checks include the shape of a matrix controls which products are valid and using the method to catch incompatible matrix and vector operations.

questions.prompt: You see multiplying matrices without checking inner dimensions in a worked solution. What does that usually indicate?
choices: The inner dimensions do not match
The final answer must be exact
The method has become independent of the data
The model no longer needs validation
questions.explanation: The warning sign is: The inner dimensions do not match.

exams.title: Week 1 Part 1 - Scalars, Vectors, and Matrices - Hard Quiz
exams.description: Weekly Lecture Exams - Higher-mark diagnostic quiz for W1-P1 - Scalars Vectors and Matrices.pdf.

questions.prompt: In storing feature values for an AI model, why is tracking dimensions before multiplying more than a notational detail?
choices: It helps to catch incompatible matrix and vector operations
It guarantees every model will generalize
It replaces checking data quality
It proves the problem has no assumptions
questions.explanation: tracking dimensions before multiplying helps to catch incompatible matrix and vector operations.

questions.prompt: Select all reliable statements related to a vector.
choices: Vectors have both entries and an order
A matrix can be interpreted as a table of numbers
Changing the order of vector entries never changes the vector
Every matrix product AB is valid
The shape of a matrix controls which products are valid
questions.explanation: The reliable statements are: Vectors have both entries and an order; A matrix can be interpreted as a table of numbers; The shape of a matrix controls which products are valid.

questions.prompt: Which answer best fixes the misconception: Multiplying matrices without checking inner dimensions?
choices: Verify that the number of columns in the left matrix equals the number of rows in the right matrix
Use the same expression but change the variable names
Round all entries before checking the math
Choose the most complex formula available
questions.explanation: Verify that the number of columns in the left matrix equals the number of rows in the right matrix

questions.prompt: The formula "If x has shape n x 1 and A has shape m x n, then Ax has shape m x 1." should be used together with its assumptions and shape requirements.
choices: True
False
questions.explanation: True. In this topic, the formula matters because shape checks prevent invalid matrix-vector products before doing calculations..

questions.prompt: A model pipeline gives suspicious results because the inner dimensions do not match. Which review question should come first?
choices: Did we correctly handle a vector and tracking dimensions before multiplying?
Can we skip validation because the output is numeric?
Can we change the target variable after training?
Can we remove all features to avoid computation?
questions.explanation: The first review should connect the diagnostic to a vector and tracking dimensions before multiplying.

questions.prompt: Which false statement is the most dangerous shortcut for this topic?
choices: Changing the order of vector entries never changes the vector
Vectors have both entries and an order
A matrix can be interpreted as a table of numbers
Shape checks prevent invalid matrix-vector products before doing calculations.
questions.explanation: Changing the order of vector entries never changes the vector is false and can lead to invalid reasoning.

questions.prompt: A classmate writes a final answer but never checks whether every matrix product ab is valid. What should you say?
choices: Verify that the number of columns in the left matrix equals the number of rows in the right matrix
Assumptions are optional in math for AI
Only the final numeric value matters
The graph of the answer is enough evidence
questions.explanation: Verify that the number of columns in the left matrix equals the number of rows in the right matrix

questions.prompt: Which two choices best connect this topic to machine learning practice?
choices: storing feature values for an AI model
to catch incompatible matrix and vector operations
A scalar must always be written as a 1 x 1 matrix
memorizing symbols without interpretation
questions.explanation: storing feature values for an AI model and the ability to catch incompatible matrix and vector operations connect the math to model practice.

questions.prompt: If a question asks for the meaning of a vector, which response is strongest?
choices: an ordered list of numbers that can represent a point, direction, feature row, or parameter set
Multiplying matrices without checking inner dimensions
Every matrix product AB is valid
a term that is useful only in handwritten proofs
questions.explanation: A vector is an ordered list of numbers that can represent a point, direction, feature row, or parameter set.

questions.prompt: A scalar must always be written as a 1 x 1 matrix.
choices: True
False
questions.explanation: False. A safer related statement is: The shape of a matrix controls which products are valid.

questions.prompt: While solving a problem from W1-P1 - Scalars Vectors and Matrices.pdf, the work reaches this checkpoint: If x has shape n x 1 and A has shape m x n, then Ax has shape m x 1.. What should be documented?
choices: Why the formula applies: Shape checks prevent invalid matrix-vector products before doing calculations.
Only the page number
Only the final decimal approximation
A promise that all formulas are interchangeable
questions.explanation: Documenting assumptions and meaning matters: Shape checks prevent invalid matrix-vector products before doing calculations..

questions.prompt: Which result would most clearly suggest that tracking dimensions before multiplying was not applied carefully?
choices: The inner dimensions do not match
Vectors have both entries and an order
A matrix can be interpreted as a table of numbers
storing feature values for an AI model
questions.explanation: A warning sign is: The inner dimensions do not match.

exams.title: Week 1 Part 2 - Matrix Operations
exams.description: Weekly Lecture Exams - Addition, scalar multiplication, matrix products, transposes, identity matrices, and common algebra rules. Source: W1-P2 - Matrix Operations.pdf.

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes matrix multiplication?
choices: a row-by-column operation that combines rows of the first matrix with columns of the second
a rule that removes the need to check assumptions
a random label assigned to a formula
a plotting convention with no algebraic meaning
questions.explanation: Matrix multiplication is a row-by-column operation that combines rows of the first matrix with columns of the second.

questions.prompt: Which statements are true for Matrix Operations?
choices: Matrix addition requires equal shapes
AB always equals BA
The identity matrix leaves compatible vectors unchanged
A + B is valid for any two matrices
questions.explanation: The true statements are: Matrix addition requires equal shapes and The identity matrix leaves compatible vectors unchanged.

questions.prompt: Assuming AB = BA because scalar multiplication commutes is a safe general strategy.
choices: True
False
questions.explanation: False. Keep matrix factor order unless a theorem specifically permits changing it.

questions.prompt: A student is working on combining linear transformations in a neural network layer. Which technique is most directly useful?
choices: using transpose and identity rules
ignoring dimensions and units
choosing a formula only because it is longer
removing all assumptions from the problem
questions.explanation: using transpose and identity rules is useful to simplify matrix expressions.

questions.prompt: What is the best interpretation of this formula: (AB)^T = B^T A^T.
choices: The transpose of a product reverses the order of the factors.
It proves that every matrix operation is valid
It says all model parameters must be zero
It removes the need for data preprocessing
questions.explanation: The transpose of a product reverses the order of the factors.

questions.prompt: A solution has this problem: The algebra silently changed the order of noncommuting matrices. What is the best correction?
choices: Keep matrix factor order unless a theorem specifically permits changing it
Continue with the same calculation and ignore the warning
Switch every vector into a scalar
Assume the final answer is correct because the notation looks familiar
questions.explanation: Keep matrix factor order unless a theorem specifically permits changing it

questions.prompt: Which ideas should you check when applying matrix multiplication in an AI or data problem?
choices: Matrix multiplication is generally not commutative
to simplify matrix expressions
(AB)^T always equals A^T B^T
Assuming AB = BA because scalar multiplication commutes
questions.explanation: Useful checks include matrix multiplication is generally not commutative and using the method to simplify matrix expressions.

questions.prompt: You see assuming ab = ba because scalar multiplication commutes in a worked solution. What does that usually indicate?
choices: The algebra silently changed the order of noncommuting matrices
The final answer must be exact
The method has become independent of the data
The model no longer needs validation
questions.explanation: The warning sign is: The algebra silently changed the order of noncommuting matrices.

exams.title: Week 1 Part 3 - Linear Systems and Elimination
exams.description: Weekly Lecture Exams - Augmented matrices, row operations, pivots, inconsistent systems, and inverse-based solving. Source: W1-P3 - Linear Systems and Elimination.pdf.

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes Gaussian elimination?
choices: a sequence of valid row operations used to transform a linear system into an easier equivalent system
a rule that removes the need to check assumptions
a random label assigned to a formula
a plotting convention with no algebraic meaning
questions.explanation: Gaussian elimination is a sequence of valid row operations used to transform a linear system into an easier equivalent system.

questions.prompt: Which statements are true for Linear Systems and Elimination?
choices: Row swaps preserve the solution set
Every linear system has exactly one solution
A pivot identifies a leading variable
Row operations change the unknowns themselves
questions.explanation: The true statements are: Row swaps preserve the solution set and A pivot identifies a leading variable.

questions.prompt: Using an inverse when the coefficient matrix is singular is a safe general strategy.
choices: True
False
questions.explanation: False. Check pivots or determinant before applying x = A^-1 b.

questions.prompt: A student is working on solving normal equations in a regression workflow. Which technique is most directly useful?
choices: row reduction of an augmented matrix
ignoring dimensions and units
choosing a formula only because it is longer
removing all assumptions from the problem
questions.explanation: row reduction of an augmented matrix is useful to solve simultaneous linear equations.

questions.prompt: What is the best interpretation of this formula: Ax = b has a unique solution when A is square and invertible.
choices: Invertibility tells whether an inverse-based solution x = A^-1 b is valid.
It proves that every matrix operation is valid
It says all model parameters must be zero
It removes the need for data preprocessing
questions.explanation: Invertibility tells whether an inverse-based solution x = A^-1 b is valid.

questions.prompt: A solution has this problem: The reduced matrix has a missing pivot or contradiction. What is the best correction?
choices: Check pivots or determinant before applying x = A^-1 b
Continue with the same calculation and ignore the warning
Switch every vector into a scalar
Assume the final answer is correct because the notation looks familiar
questions.explanation: Check pivots or determinant before applying x = A^-1 b

questions.prompt: Which ideas should you check when applying Gaussian elimination in an AI or data problem?
choices: A row like 0 = 5 signals inconsistency
to solve simultaneous linear equations
A zero row always means no solution
Using an inverse when the coefficient matrix is singular
questions.explanation: Useful checks include a row like 0 = 5 signals inconsistency and using the method to solve simultaneous linear equations.

questions.prompt: You see using an inverse when the coefficient matrix is singular in a worked solution. What does that usually indicate?
choices: The reduced matrix has a missing pivot or contradiction
The final answer must be exact
The method has become independent of the data
The model no longer needs validation
questions.explanation: The warning sign is: The reduced matrix has a missing pivot or contradiction.

exams.title: Week 2 Part 1 - Vector Spaces and Subspaces
exams.description: Weekly Lecture Exams - Vector space rules, subspace tests, closure, span, and examples used in machine learning. Source: W2-P1 - Vector Spaces and Subspaces.pdf.

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes a subspace?
choices: a nonempty set of vectors closed under addition and scalar multiplication
a rule that removes the need to check assumptions
a random label assigned to a formula
a plotting convention with no algebraic meaning
questions.explanation: A subspace is a nonempty set of vectors closed under addition and scalar multiplication.

questions.prompt: Which statements are true for Vector Spaces and Subspaces?
choices: A subspace must contain the zero vector
Any set of points is a subspace
A span is always a subspace
A subspace can exclude the zero vector
questions.explanation: The true statements are: A subspace must contain the zero vector and A span is always a subspace.

questions.prompt: Calling a shifted line through a nonzero point a subspace is a safe general strategy.
choices: True
False
questions.explanation: False. Confirm the set passes through the origin and is closed under the two operations.

questions.prompt: A student is working on describing the feature combinations a linear model can express. Which technique is most directly useful?
choices: checking the subspace closure tests
ignoring dimensions and units
choosing a formula only because it is longer
removing all assumptions from the problem
questions.explanation: checking the subspace closure tests is useful to decide whether a candidate set is a valid linear space.

questions.prompt: What is the best interpretation of this formula: span(v1, ..., vk) is the set of all linear combinations c1v1 + ... + ckvk.
choices: Span describes every vector reachable from a chosen set of building blocks.
It proves that every matrix operation is valid
It says all model parameters must be zero
It removes the need for data preprocessing
questions.explanation: Span describes every vector reachable from a chosen set of building blocks.

questions.prompt: A solution has this problem: The set fails to include the zero vector. What is the best correction?
choices: Confirm the set passes through the origin and is closed under the two operations
Continue with the same calculation and ignore the warning
Switch every vector into a scalar
Assume the final answer is correct because the notation looks familiar
questions.explanation: Confirm the set passes through the origin and is closed under the two operations

questions.prompt: Which ideas should you check when applying a subspace in an AI or data problem?
choices: Closure under scalar multiplication is required
to decide whether a candidate set is a valid linear space
Closure under addition alone is enough
Calling a shifted line through a nonzero point a subspace
questions.explanation: Useful checks include closure under scalar multiplication is required and using the method to decide whether a candidate set is a valid linear space.

questions.prompt: You see calling a shifted line through a nonzero point a subspace in a worked solution. What does that usually indicate?
choices: The set fails to include the zero vector
The final answer must be exact
The method has become independent of the data
The model no longer needs validation
questions.explanation: The warning sign is: The set fails to include the zero vector.

exams.title: Week 2 Part 2 - Linear Independence, Basis, and Dimension
exams.description: Weekly Lecture Exams - Independence tests, basis construction, dimension, rank, and redundant features. Source: W2-P2 - Linear Independence Basis and Dimension.pdf.

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes a basis?
choices: a linearly independent set that spans a vector space
a rule that removes the need to check assumptions
a random label assigned to a formula
a plotting convention with no algebraic meaning
questions.explanation: A basis is a linearly independent set that spans a vector space.

questions.prompt: Which statements are true for Linear Independence, Basis, and Dimension?
choices: A basis spans the whole space
A spanning set is automatically a basis
A basis has no redundant vector
A basis can contain any number of redundant vectors
questions.explanation: The true statements are: A basis spans the whole space and A basis has no redundant vector.

questions.prompt: Treating a dependent spanning set as a basis is a safe general strategy.
choices: True
False
questions.explanation: False. Remove redundant vectors until the remaining spanning set is independent.

questions.prompt: A student is working on reducing duplicated input features before model training. Which technique is most directly useful?
choices: testing whether a vector is a combination of the others
ignoring dimensions and units

a plotting convention with no algebraic meaning

questions.prompt:
### Task 1 — One-Sample z-test (10 marks)

A manufacturer claims their batteries have a mean lifetime of $\mu_0 = 500$ hours with
known standard deviation $\sigma = 25$ hours. A consumer group tests 40 batteries and
finds a sample mean of $\bar{x} = 492$ hours.

1. State $H_0$ and $H_1$ (two-sided).
2. Compute the z-statistic and the two-sided p-value.
3. State the critical values at $\alpha = 0.05$.
4. Make the decision and interpret the result in context.
questions.code:
# --- SOLUTION: Task 1 ---
mu_0 = 500
sigma = 25
n = 40
x_bar = 492
alpha = 0.05

se = sigma / np.sqrt(n)
z = (x_bar - mu_0) / se
p_value = 2 * (1 - stats.norm.cdf(abs(z)))
z_crit = stats.norm.ppf(1 - alpha/2)

print("Step 1: H_0: μ = 500   vs   H_1: μ ≠ 500  (two-sided)")
print(f"Step 2: α = {alpha}")
print(f"Step 3: Z = (X̄ - μ_0)/(σ/√n)")
print(f"Step 4: SE = {sigma}/√{n} = {se:.4f}")
print(f"        Z_obs = ({x_bar} - {mu_0})/{se:.4f} = {z:.4f}")
print(f"Step 5: Critical values ±{z_crit:.4f}")
print(f"        p-value = {p_value:.4f}")
print(f"Step 6: |{z:.4f}| > {z_crit:.4f}? {'YES → REJECT H_0' if abs(z) > z_crit else 'NO → fail to reject'}")
print(f"Step 7: At α = 0.05, the data provide significant evidence that the true mean")
print(f"        battery lifetime differs from the manufacturer's claim of 500 hours.")

A z-test compares a sample mean with a claimed population mean when the population standard deviation is known.

questions.prompt:
### Task 2 — One-Sample t-test (10 marks)

The cholesterol levels (mg/dL) of 14 patients are measured:

```
[218, 195, 240, 232, 207, 251, 199, 226, 245, 215, 209, 234, 222, 218]
```

The healthy threshold is 200 mg/dL. Use a one-sample t-test to determine whether the
mean cholesterol is significantly **greater than** 200 (right-tailed test, $\alpha = 0.05$).

1. Compute $\bar{x}$, $s$, the t-statistic, and degrees of freedom.
2. Compute the one-sided p-value.
3. State the critical value and make the decision.
4. Verify your result using `scipy.stats.ttest_1samp`.
questions.code:
# --- SOLUTION: Task 2 ---
data = np.array([218, 195, 240, 232, 207, 251, 199, 226, 245, 215, 209, 234, 222, 218])
mu_0 = 200
alpha = 0.05

n = len(data)
x_bar = np.mean(data)
s = np.std(data, ddof=1)
se = s / np.sqrt(n)
t_stat = (x_bar - mu_0) / se
df = n - 1

# One-sided (right-tailed)
p_value = 1 - stats.t.cdf(t_stat, df)
t_crit = stats.t.ppf(1 - alpha, df)

print(f"Sample: n = {n}, x̄ = {x_bar:.4f}, s = {s:.4f}")
print()
print("Step 1: H_0: μ ≤ 200   vs   H_1: μ > 200  (right-tailed)")
print(f"Step 2: α = {alpha}")
print(f"Step 3: T = (X̄ - μ_0)/(S/√n) ~ t(n-1)")
print(f"Step 4: SE = {se:.4f}")
print(f"        T_obs = ({x_bar:.4f} - {mu_0})/{se:.4f} = {t_stat:.4f}")
print(f"Step 5: df = {df},  Critical value = {t_crit:.4f}")
print(f"        One-sided p-value = {p_value:.4f}")
print(f"Step 6: T_obs > {t_crit:.4f}? {'YES → REJECT H_0' if t_stat > t_crit else 'NO'}")
print()

# Verify with scipy (returns two-sided)
sp_t, sp_p_two = stats.ttest_1samp(data, mu_0)
sp_p_one = sp_p_two / 2 if sp_t > 0 else 1 - sp_p_two/2
print(f"Verification: scipy t = {sp_t:.4f}")
print(f"  Two-sided p = {sp_p_two:.4f}, one-sided p = {sp_p_one:.4f}  ✓")

questions.prompt:
### Task 3 — Independent Two-Sample t-test (12 marks)

Two groups of students take the same exam after being taught with different methods:

- **Method A (n = 18):** mean = 73.4, sd = 9.1
- **Method B (n = 22):** mean = 79.1, sd = 8.6

1. Compute the pooled standard deviation $S_p$.
2. Compute the test statistic and degrees of freedom (assume equal variances).
3. Compute the two-sided p-value at $\alpha = 0.05$.
4. Make the decision and interpret.

(You will need to work from summary statistics here — no raw data.)
questions.code:
# --- SOLUTION: Task 3 ---
n1, x1, s1 = 18, 73.4, 9.1
n2, x2, s2 = 22, 79.1, 8.6
alpha = 0.05

# Pooled standard deviation
sp = np.sqrt(((n1 - 1)*s1**2 + (n2 - 1)*s2**2) / (n1 + n2 - 2))
se_diff = sp * np.sqrt(1/n1 + 1/n2)
t_stat = (x1 - x2) / se_diff
df = n1 + n2 - 2
p_value = 2 * (1 - stats.t.cdf(abs(t_stat), df))
t_crit = stats.t.ppf(1 - alpha/2, df)

print("Pooled t-test (equal variances)")
print("-" * 40)
print(f"S_p = √((17×9.1² + 21×8.6²)/38) = {sp:.4f}")
print(f"SE(diff) = {sp:.4f} × √(1/18 + 1/22) = {se_diff:.4f}")
print(f"T = ({x1} - {x2})/{se_diff:.4f} = {t_stat:.4f}")
print(f"df = 18 + 22 - 2 = {df}")
print(f"Critical values: ±{t_crit:.4f}")
print(f"p-value = {p_value:.4f}")
print(f"Decision: {'REJECT' if abs(t_stat) > t_crit else 'FAIL TO REJECT'} H_0")
print()

# Verify
sp_t, sp_p = stats.ttest_ind_from_stats(x1, s1, n1, x2, s2, n2, equal_var=True)
print(f"Verification: scipy t = {sp_t:.4f}, p = {sp_p:.4f}  ✓")
print()
print("Method B produces significantly higher exam scores at α = 0.05.")

questions.prompt:
### Task 4 — Welch's t-test (12 marks)

Two A/B test variants of a website are tested. Variant A serves 30 users with conversion
times having mean 12.4 s and sd 2.1 s. Variant B serves 25 users with mean 11.6 s and
sd 4.5 s.

1. Verify (informally) that the variances differ enough to warrant Welch's test.
2. Compute Welch's t-statistic.
3. Compute the Welch–Satterthwaite degrees of freedom.
4. Compute the p-value (two-sided) and decide at $\alpha = 0.05$.
5. Compare your manual result to `scipy.stats.ttest_ind_from_stats(equal_var=False)`.
questions.code:
# --- SOLUTION: Task 4 ---
n1, x1, s1 = 30, 12.4, 2.1
n2, x2, s2 = 25, 11.6, 4.5
alpha = 0.05

# Variance ratio check
print(f"Variance check: s_2²/s_1² = {(s2/s1)**2:.4f}")
print("Variances differ by ~4.5× — Welch's test is appropriate.")
print()

# Welch's test
se_welch = np.sqrt(s1**2/n1 + s2**2/n2)
t_stat = (x1 - x2) / se_welch

# Welch–Satterthwaite df
num = (s1**2/n1 + s2**2/n2)**2
den = (s1**2/n1)**2/(n1-1) + (s2**2/n2)**2/(n2-1)
df_welch = num / den
p_value = 2 * (1 - stats.t.cdf(abs(t_stat), df_welch))

print("Welch's t-test")
print("-" * 40)
print(f"SE = √({s1}²/{n1} + {s2}²/{n2}) = {se_welch:.4f}")
print(f"T = ({x1} - {x2})/{se_welch:.4f} = {t_stat:.4f}")
print(f"Welch–Satterthwaite df = {df_welch:.4f}")
print(f"p-value = {p_value:.4f}")
print(f"Decision: {'REJECT' if p_value < alpha else 'FAIL TO REJECT'} H_0")
print()

# Verify
sp_t, sp_p = stats.ttest_ind_from_stats(x1, s1, n1, x2, s2, n2, equal_var=False)
print(f"Verification: scipy t = {sp_t:.4f}, p = {sp_p:.4f}  ✓")

questions.prompt:
### Task 5 — Paired t-test (12 marks)

Ten students take a test before and after a study workshop:

```
Before: [62, 71, 58, 65, 70, 55, 68, 73, 60, 66]
After:  [70, 75, 64, 68, 76, 62, 73, 78, 65, 71]
```

1. Compute the differences $D_i = \text{After}_i - \text{Before}_i$.
2. Compute the t-statistic for the paired test.
3. Test (right-tailed) whether the workshop *improves* scores at $\alpha = 0.01$.
4. Verify with `scipy.stats.ttest_rel`.
5. For comparison, run an independent-samples t-test on the same data and explain why
   the paired test is more appropriate (and more powerful) here.
questions.code:
# --- SOLUTION: Task 5 ---
before = np.array([62, 71, 58, 65, 70, 55, 68, 73, 60, 66])
after  = np.array([70, 75, 64, 68, 76, 62, 73, 78, 65, 71])
alpha = 0.01

# Differences
d = after - before  # positive = improvement
n = len(d)
d_bar = np.mean(d)
s_d = np.std(d, ddof=1)
se_d = s_d / np.sqrt(n)
t_stat = d_bar / se_d
df = n - 1

# Right-tailed
p_one = 1 - stats.t.cdf(t_stat, df)
t_crit = stats.t.ppf(1 - alpha, df)

print(f"Differences: {d}")
print(f"D̄ = {d_bar:.4f}, S_D = {s_d:.4f}, SE = {se_d:.4f}")
print()
print("Paired t-test (right-tailed: H_1: μ_d > 0)")
print(f"  T = {t_stat:.4f},  df = {df}")
print(f"  Critical value (α=0.01) = {t_crit:.4f}")
print(f"  One-sided p-value = {p_one:.6f}")
print(f"  Decision: {'REJECT' if t_stat > t_crit else 'FAIL TO REJECT'} H_0")
print()

# Verify
sp_t, sp_p_two = stats.ttest_rel(after, before)
print(f"Verification (two-sided): scipy t = {sp_t:.4f}, p = {sp_p_two:.6f}")
print(f"Manual one-sided p = {p_one:.6f} ≈ {sp_p_two/2:.6f}  ✓")
print()

# Compare with independent t-test
ind_t, ind_p = stats.ttest_ind(after, before)
print(f"INCORRECT independent t-test: t = {ind_t:.4f}, p = {ind_p:.4f}")
print()
print("The paired test is appropriate here because the same students were measured")
print("twice. Pairing removes the between-subject variability, leading to a much")
print("smaller standard error and a more powerful test (much smaller p-value).")
questions.modelAnswer:
---
## 9. Reflection and Next Steps

### What We Covered Today

This lab built a comprehensive foundation for **statistical decision-making**:

- **Hypothesis testing** as a formal framework for choosing between competing claims using
  sample data.
- **Null and alternative hypotheses**, and the choice between **one-tailed and two-tailed**
  tests based on the research question.
- **Critical regions and decision rules**, computed both via critical values and via p-values.
- **Type I and Type II errors** — the two ways a test can be wrong — and their trade-off
  through significance level and power.
- The complete **z-test** and the family of **t-tests**: one-sample, two-sample independent,
  paired, and Welch's variant.
- **Degrees of freedom** for each test and the rationale for the **Welch–Satterthwaite**
  formula when variances are unequal.
- The **decision guide** for choosing the right test in any practical scenario.

### Connection to AI

Hypothesis testing is the workhorse of empirical AI research:
- **Model comparison** (paired t-test on per-example outcomes) — exactly what we did in
  Section 6.
- **A/B testing** in deployed systems (recommender systems, ad ranking, UI changes).
- **Validating fairness** (testing whether accuracy is equal across demographic groups).
- **Reproducibility** in academic ML papers — proper statistical testing is increasingly
  required by venues such as NeurIPS and ICML.

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid

1. **Misinterpreting the p-value:** It is *not* the probability that $H_0$ is true.
2. **Confusing statistical and practical significance:** A tiny effect can be highly
   significant with a huge sample, but may not matter in practice.
3. **Multiple testing:** Running many tests inflates the chance of false positives —
   adjust with Bonferroni, FDR, etc.
4. **Using the wrong test:** Treating paired data as independent (or vice versa) gives
   misleading conclusions.
5. **Ignoring assumptions:** Welch's t-test is the safer default for two-sample comparisons.


### Reflection Questions (not assessed)

1. A research paper reports "p = 0.049, the new method is significantly better."
   What further information would you want before believing this claim?
2. Why do we prefer to use $\alpha = 0.05$ as a default? What are the implications of
   using a stricter ($\alpha = 0.01$) or looser ($\alpha = 0.10$) threshold?
3. Under what conditions would a paired t-test be **less** powerful than an independent
   t-test on the same data?
4. If a colleague says "our model has 91 % accuracy and theirs has 90 %, so ours is better,"
   what is missing from this claim?

---

**End of Week 8 Lab — Well done!** 🎉

*If you found any errors or have suggestions, please raise them in the next seminar session.*

questions.prompt:
### Task 1 — Set Operations (10 marks)

Three sensors monitor a factory. Each records the IDs of defective items it detects:

```
Sensor A: {101, 102, 105, 107, 110, 112}
Sensor B: {102, 103, 107, 108, 112}
Sensor C: {101, 105, 108, 110, 112, 115}
```

1. Compute: $A \cup B \cup C$, $A \cap B \cap C$, items detected by **exactly two** sensors.
2. If the universal set $U = \{101, 102, \ldots, 120\}$, find items detected by **no** sensor.
3. Use the inclusion–exclusion formula to verify $|A \cup B \cup C|$.
4. Interpret the results in a quality-control context.
questions.code:
# --- SOLUTION: Task 1 ---
A = {101, 102, 105, 107, 110, 112}
B = {102, 103, 107, 108, 112}
C = {101, 105, 108, 110, 112, 115}
U = set(range(101, 121))

union_all = A | B | C
inter_all = A & B & C

print(f"A ∪ B ∪ C = {sorted(union_all)}")
print(f"A ∩ B ∩ C = {sorted(inter_all)}")
print()

# Exactly two sensors: in AB but not C, or AC but not B, or BC but not A
ab_only = (A & B) - C
ac_only = (A & C) - B
bc_only = (B & C) - A
exactly_two = ab_only | ac_only | bc_only
print(f"Detected by exactly 2 sensors: {sorted(exactly_two)}")
print(f"  A∩B only: {sorted(ab_only)}")
print(f"  A∩C only: {sorted(ac_only)}")
print(f"  B∩C only: {sorted(bc_only)}")
print()

# No sensor
no_sensor = U - union_all
print(f"Detected by NO sensor: {sorted(no_sensor)}")
print()

# Inclusion-exclusion
ie = len(A) + len(B) + len(C) - len(A&B) - len(A&C) - len(B&C) + len(A&B&C)
print(f"Inclusion-exclusion: |A|+|B|+|C| - |A∩B| - |A∩C| - |B∩C| + |A∩B∩C|")
print(f"  = {len(A)}+{len(B)}+{len(C)} - {len(A&B)} - {len(A&C)} - {len(B&C)} + {len(A&B&C)} = {ie}")
print(f"  |A ∪ B ∪ C| = {len(union_all)}  ✓")
print()
print("Quality control: Items 112 is detected by all 3 sensors — high confidence defective.")
print("Items detected by only 1 sensor may need re-inspection.")

questions.prompt:
### Task 2 — Logic and Truth Tables (10 marks)

A credit card fraud detection system uses these rules:

- $p$: Transaction amount > £5000
- $q$: Transaction is international
- $r$: Card was used in the last hour

The fraud alert triggers when: $(p \land q) \lor (p \land \lnot r)$

1. Build the complete truth table for all 8 combinations of $p$, $q$, $r$.
2. List which combinations trigger the fraud alert.
3. Express the fraud rule in plain English.
4. Identify a scenario where the rule might produce a false positive.
questions.code:
# --- SOLUTION: Task 2 ---
print("Fraud Detection Rule: (p ∧ q) ∨ (p ∧ ¬r)")
print()
print(f"{'p':>5} {'q':>5} {'r':>5}  {'p∧q':>5} {'¬r':>5} {'p∧¬r':>5} {'Alert':>7}")
print("-" * 42)

alerts = []
for p in [True, False]:
    for q in [True, False]:
        for r in [True, False]:
            pq = p and q
            not_r = not r
            p_notr = p and not_r
            alert = pq or p_notr
            print(f"{str(p):>5} {str(q):>5} {str(r):>5}  {str(pq):>5} {str(not_r):>5} "
                  f"{str(p_notr):>5} {str(alert):>7}")
            if alert:
                alerts.append((p, q, r))

print(f"\nFraud alert triggers in {len(alerts)} of 8 cases:")
for p, q, r in alerts:
    print(f"  p={p}, q={q}, r={r}")

print()
print("In English: 'Alert if the amount is large AND (the transaction is international")
print("OR the card was NOT used recently).'")
print()
print("False positive: A large domestic purchase on a card unused for hours")
print("(e.g. buying furniture while on holiday) would trigger the alert.")

questions.prompt:
### Task 3 — Graph Representation and Adjacency Matrix (12 marks)

Consider a directed graph with 5 nodes: A, B, C, D, E and edges:
A→B, A→C, B→D, C→D, D→E, E→A, C→E.

1. Construct the adjacency matrix $A$.
2. Compute $A^2$ and interpret what $A^2_{ij}$ tells you.
3. Verify: how many paths of length 2 go from A to D?
4. Compute the in-degree and out-degree of each node.
questions.code:
# --- SOLUTION: Task 3 ---
nodes = ['A', 'B', 'C', 'D', 'E']
n = len(nodes)
A_mat = np.zeros((n, n), dtype=int)
dir_edges = [('A','B'), ('A','C'), ('B','D'), ('C','D'), ('D','E'), ('E','A'), ('C','E')]

for u, v in dir_edges:
    i, j = nodes.index(u), nodes.index(v)
    A_mat[i, j] = 1

print("Adjacency Matrix (Directed):")
print(f"     {'  '.join(nodes)}")
for i, name in enumerate(nodes):
    print(f"  {name}  {A_mat[i]}")
print()

# A²
A2 = A_mat @ A_mat
print("A² (paths of length 2):")
print(f"     {'  '.join(nodes)}")
for i, name in enumerate(nodes):
    print(f"  {name}  {A2[i]}")
print()

# A to D
print(f"Paths of length 2 from A to D: {A2[0, 3]}")
print("  A→B→D and A→C→D")
print()

# Degrees
out_deg = A_mat.sum(axis=1)
in_deg = A_mat.sum(axis=0)
print(f"{'Node':>6} {'Out-degree':>12} {'In-degree':>11}")
print("-" * 31)
for i, name in enumerate(nodes):
    print(f"{name:>6} {out_deg[i]:>12} {in_deg[i]:>11}")

questions.prompt:
### Task 4 — Combinatorics and Feature Selection (10 marks)

A machine learning dataset has 15 features. You want to select the best subset.

1. How many possible subsets of size 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 are there? Compute each using
   $C(15, k)$.
2. How many total subsets exist (including the empty set)?
3. If evaluating one subset takes 2 seconds, how long would exhaustive search take?
4. Given this, explain why greedy or random search methods are preferred.
questions.code:
# --- SOLUTION: Task 4 ---
n = 15

print(f"Feature Selection from {n} features")
print("-" * 40)
total = 0
for k in range(1, 6):
    c = comb(n, k)
    total += c
    print(f"  C({n},{k}) = {c}")
print(f"  Subsets of size 1-5: {total}")
print()

total_all = 2**n
print(f"Total subsets (including empty): 2^{n} = {total_all:,}")
print()

time_per = 2  # seconds
total_time = total_all * time_per
print(f"Exhaustive search time: {total_all:,} × {time_per}s = {total_time:,}s = {total_time/3600:.1f} hours")
print()
print("With 15 features, exhaustive search takes ~18 hours — feasible but slow.")
print("With 50 features, 2^50 ≈ 10^15 subsets → millions of years!")
print("This is why greedy methods (forward/backward selection) or random search")
print("are essential in practice.")

questions.prompt:
### Task 5 — Knowledge Graph Querying (10 marks)

Build a knowledge graph (as a list of triples) for a small university domain containing
at least 12 triples. Include entities like courses, lecturers, departments, and buildings.

1. Print all triples.
2. Write a function that answers: "What does [entity] teach?"
3. Write a function for 2-hop queries: "Which building houses the department of [lecturer]?"
4. Discuss how this type of structured knowledge could enhance an AI chatbot.
questions.code:
# --- SOLUTION: Task 5 ---
kg = [
    ('Dr Smith',    'teaches',      'AI Maths'),
    ('Dr Smith',    'belongs_to',   'CS Department'),
    ('Dr Jones',    'teaches',      'Databases'),
    ('Dr Jones',    'belongs_to',   'CS Department'),
    ('Dr Brown',    'teaches',      'Statistics'),
    ('Dr Brown',    'belongs_to',   'Maths Department'),
    ('CS Department',    'housed_in', 'Babbage Building'),
    ('Maths Department', 'housed_in', 'Newton Building'),
    ('AI Maths',    'is_a',         'Module'),
    ('Databases',   'is_a',         'Module'),
    ('Statistics',  'is_a',         'Module'),
    ('AI Maths',    'requires',     'Statistics'),
]

print("University Knowledge Graph")
print("=" * 55)
for s, r, o in kg:
    print(f"  ({s}) —[{r}]→ ({o})")

def what_teaches(kg, lecturer):
    return [o for s, r, o in kg if s == lecturer and r == 'teaches']

def building_of_lecturer(kg, lecturer):
    # Hop 1: lecturer → belongs_to → department
    depts = [o for s, r, o in kg if s == lecturer and r == 'belongs_to']
    # Hop 2: department → housed_in → building
    buildings = [o for s, r, o in kg if s in depts and r == 'housed_in']
    return buildings

print()
for lec in ['Dr Smith', 'Dr Brown']:
    courses = what_teaches(kg, lec)
    buildings = building_of_lecturer(kg, lec)
    print(f"  {lec} teaches: {courses}")
    print(f"  {lec}'s building (2-hop): {buildings}")
    print()

print("A chatbot enhanced with this knowledge graph could answer:")
print("  'Where is Dr Smith's office?' → Babbage Building (via CS Department)")
print("  'What do I need before taking AI Maths?' → Statistics (via requires edge)")
print("This structured knowledge avoids hallucination and grounds the AI in facts.")
questions.modelAnswer:
---
## 10. Reflection and Next Steps

### What We Covered Today

This lab explored four pillars of **discrete mathematics** and their connections to AI:

- **Set Theory** gave us a formal language for datasets, classes, features, and filtering
  operations — the very foundation of data manipulation.
- **Propositional Logic** underpins rule-based systems, decision trees, and explainable AI.
  We saw both its power (transparency, interpretability) and its limitations (brittleness,
  inability to handle uncertainty or learn from data).
- **Graph Theory** provided the mathematical framework for modelling relationships —
  social networks, the web, knowledge bases, and recommender systems. Adjacency matrices
  connected graphs back to linear algebra, and shortest-path algorithms are fundamental
  to AI search.
- **Combinatorics** quantified the scale of AI problems: the number of possible feature
  subsets, input patterns, and hyperparameter configurations. The **combinatorial explosion**
  is one of the fundamental challenges that makes brute-force approaches infeasible and
  motivates the need for intelligent search and learning algorithms.

### Connection to AI

| Discrete Maths Concept | AI Application |
|---|---|
| Sets | Data splits, feature selection, class labels |
| Logic | Decision trees, rule engines, XAI |
| Graphs | Knowledge graphs, recommender systems, neural architectures |
| Combinatorics | Hyperparameter search, NAS, feature engineering |


### Reflection Questions (not assessed)

1. Give an example from your own experience where set operations could simplify a data
   processing task.
2. Why are decision trees considered more "explainable" than neural networks, even though
   both can achieve similar accuracy?
3. If a knowledge graph has $n$ entity types and $m$ relationship types, how many possible
   triple types $(e_i, r_k, e_j)$ exist? What does this tell you about knowledge graph
   completeness?
4. A colleague proposes testing all possible subsets of 30 features. Explain mathematically
   why this is infeasible and suggest an alternative.

---

**End of Week 10 Lab — Well done!** 🎉

*If you found any errors or have suggestions, please raise them in the next seminar session.*

questions.prompt:
### Task 1 — Floating-Point Precision Exploration (10 marks)

1. Compute $\sqrt{2}$ in FP64, FP32, and FP16. Print the absolute and relative errors
   (using FP64 as "truth").
2. Verify that $1 + \varepsilon > 1$ for machine epsilon $\varepsilon$ but $1 + \varepsilon/2 = 1$
   in FP32 and FP64.
3. Find the smallest integer $n$ such that $n + 1 = n$ in FP32 (i.e. adding 1 is lost).
4. Explain why this matters for loop counters in GPU computations.
questions.code:
# --- SOLUTION: Task 1 ---
import math

sqrt2_64 = np.float64(math.sqrt(2))
sqrt2_32 = np.float32(math.sqrt(2))
sqrt2_16 = np.float16(math.sqrt(2))

print("√2 in different precisions:")
for name, val in [('FP64', sqrt2_64), ('FP32', sqrt2_32), ('FP16', sqrt2_16)]:
    abs_err = abs(float(val) - float(sqrt2_64))
    rel_err = abs_err / float(sqrt2_64) if name != 'FP64' else 0
    print(f"  {name}: {float(val):.20f}  abs_err={abs_err:.2e}  rel_err={rel_err:.2e}")
print()

# Machine epsilon verification
for dtype, name in [(np.float32, 'FP32'), (np.float64, 'FP64')]:
    eps = np.finfo(dtype).eps
    one = dtype(1.0)
    print(f"{name}: ε = {eps:.2e}")
    print(f"  1 + ε > 1? {one + dtype(eps) > one}")
    print(f"  1 + ε/2 == 1? {one + dtype(eps/2) == one}")
print()

# Smallest n where n+1 == n in FP32
n = np.float32(1.0)
while np.float32(n + 1) != n:
    n *= 2
print(f"Smallest power-of-2 n where n+1 == n in FP32: n = {n:.0f} = 2^{int(np.log2(n))}")
print("This means FP32 loop counters above ~16 million cannot count by 1!")

questions.prompt:
### Task 2 — Catastrophic Cancellation (10 marks)

The quadratic formula is: $x = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$

For $a = 1$, $b = -10^8$, $c = 1$, the smaller root should be approximately $10^{-8}$.

1. Compute both roots using the standard formula in FP64. Is the smaller root accurate?
2. Explain why catastrophic cancellation occurs in the smaller root.
3. Use the alternative formula $x = \frac{2c}{-b \mp \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}$ to compute the
   smaller root stably.
4. Compare the results.
questions.code:
# --- SOLUTION: Task 2 ---
a, b, c = 1.0, -1e8, 1.0
disc = np.sqrt(b**2 - 4*a*c)

# Standard formula
x1_std = (-b + disc) / (2*a)
x2_std = (-b - disc) / (2*a)

# Alternative formula for small root
x2_alt = (2*c) / (-b + disc)

# True values (symbolic): x = (1e8 ± √(1e16 - 4)) / 2
x1_true = 1e8
x2_true = 1e-8

print("Quadratic: x² - 10⁸x + 1 = 0")
print(f"  Discriminant = √(b² - 4ac) = {disc:.20f}")
print()
print(f"Standard formula:")
print(f"  x1 = {x1_std:.20f}  (true ≈ {x1_true})")
print(f"  x2 = {x2_std:.20e}  (true ≈ {x2_true})")
print(f"  x2 relative error = {abs(x2_std - x2_true)/x2_true:.2e}")
print()
print(f"Alternative formula for x2:")
print(f"  x2 = 2c/(-b + √disc) = {x2_alt:.20e}")
print(f"  x2 relative error = {abs(x2_alt - x2_true)/x2_true:.2e}")
print()
print("Cancellation occurs because -b = 10⁸ and √disc ≈ 10⁸ − 2×10⁻⁸,")
print("so their difference loses all significant digits.")
print("The alternative formula avoids this subtraction.")

questions.prompt:
### Task 3 — Stable Softmax Implementation (12 marks)

1. Implement both naive and stable softmax functions.
2. Test them on: $z = [10, 20, 30]$, $z = [500, 501, 502]$, $z = [-700, -699, -698]$.
3. For each case, show that the stable version produces valid probabilities (sum to 1,
   all non-negative) while the naive version fails.
4. Implement `log_softmax_stable(z)` that returns $\log(\text{softmax}(z))$ without ever
   computing softmax explicitly.
questions.code:
# --- SOLUTION: Task 3 ---
def softmax_naive(z):
    e = np.exp(z)
    return e / np.sum(e)

def softmax_stable(z):
    z_s = z - np.max(z)
    e = np.exp(z_s)
    return e / np.sum(e)

def log_softmax_stable(z):
    c = np.max(z)
    lse = c + np.log(np.sum(np.exp(z - c)))
    return z - lse

test_cases = [
    ('Normal', np.array([10.0, 20.0, 30.0])),
    ('Large', np.array([500.0, 501.0, 502.0])),
    ('Very Negative', np.array([-700.0, -699.0, -698.0])),
]

for name, z in test_cases:
    print(f"Case: {name},  z = {z}")
    with np.errstate(all='ignore'):
        naive = softmax_naive(z)
        stable = softmax_stable(z)
        log_sm = log_softmax_stable(z)

    naive_ok = np.all(np.isfinite(naive)) and np.isclose(np.sum(naive), 1.0)
    stable_ok = np.all(np.isfinite(stable)) and np.isclose(np.sum(stable), 1.0)

    print(f"  Naive:  {naive}  valid={naive_ok}")
    print(f"  Stable: {stable}  valid={stable_ok}")
    print(f"  Log-softmax: {log_sm}")
    print()

questions.prompt:
### Task 4 — Log-Probability Computation (10 marks)

A language model assigns probabilities to each word in a 100-word sentence.
Each word probability is between 0.001 and 0.05 (drawn uniformly).

1. Compute the sentence probability by direct multiplication. Does it underflow?
2. Compute the log-probability by summing log-probabilities. Print the result.
3. Compute the **perplexity** of the sentence: $\text{PPL} = \exp(-\frac{1}{N}\sum \log p_i)$.
4. Explain why perplexity is a more interpretable measure than raw probability.
questions.code:
# --- SOLUTION: Task 4 ---
np.random.seed(42)
N = 100
probs = np.random.uniform(0.001, 0.05, size=N)

# Direct multiplication
product = np.prod(probs)
print(f"Direct probability: {product}")
print(f"  → {'UNDERFLOWED to 0!' if product == 0 else 'Finite'}")
print()

# Log-space
log_prob = np.sum(np.log(probs))
print(f"Log-probability: {log_prob:.4f}")
print(f"  → Finite and accurate ✓")
print()

# Perplexity
avg_log_prob = log_prob / N
perplexity = np.exp(-avg_log_prob)
print(f"Average log-prob per word: {avg_log_prob:.4f}")
print(f"Perplexity = exp(-avg_log_prob) = {perplexity:.2f}")
print()
print("Perplexity ≈ 'effective number of equally likely choices per word'.")
print("A perplexity of 40 means the model is as uncertain as choosing from 40 options.")
print("Lower perplexity = better model. Raw probability is uninterpretable for long sequences.")

questions.prompt:
### Task 5 — Conditioning and Ill-Conditioned Systems (10 marks)

Consider the **Hilbert matrix** $H_n$ where $H_{ij} = 1/(i + j - 1)$ — a notoriously
ill-conditioned matrix.

1. Construct $H_n$ for $n = 3, 5, 8, 10, 12$.
2. Compute the condition number $\kappa(H_n)$ for each.
3. For $n = 10$, solve $Hx = b$ with $b = H \cdot [1, 1, \ldots, 1]^T$ (so the true solution
   is all ones). Print the computed solution and the error.
4. Comment on why ill-conditioned matrices are problematic in linear regression.
questions.code:
# --- SOLUTION: Task 5 ---
from scipy.linalg import hilbert

print("Hilbert Matrix Condition Numbers")
print(f"{'n':>4} {'κ(H_n)':>20}")
print("-" * 26)
for n in [3, 5, 8, 10, 12]:
    H = hilbert(n)
    kappa = np.linalg.cond(H)
    print(f"{n:>4} {kappa:>20.2e}")

print()

# Solve H_10 x = b where true x = [1, 1, ..., 1]
n = 10
H = hilbert(n)
x_true = np.ones(n)
b = H @ x_true

x_computed = np.linalg.solve(H, b)
error = np.linalg.norm(x_computed - x_true) / np.linalg.norm(x_true)

print(f"Solving H_{{10}} x = b (true solution: all ones)")
print(f"  Computed solution: {np.round(x_computed, 4)}")
print(f"  Relative error: {error:.2e}")
print(f"  Condition number: {np.linalg.cond(H):.2e}")
print()
print("Despite solving an exact system, the answer has significant errors")
print("because κ(H_10) ≈ 10¹³ — the matrix amplifies rounding errors enormously.")
print("In linear regression, X^T X can become ill-conditioned with correlated features,")
print("which is why regularisation (Ridge regression) is used to improve conditioning.")
questions.modelAnswer:
---
## 10. Reflection and Next Steps

### What We Covered Today

This lab revealed the hidden world of **numerical computation** that lies beneath every AI
system:

- **Floating-point representation** means computers inherently approximate real numbers,
  with precision determined by the format (FP64, FP32, FP16, BF16, INT8).
- **Rounding errors** are unavoidable; what matters is whether they **accumulate** or
  **cancel catastrophically**.
- **Overflow and underflow** are not theoretical — they occur routinely in softmax, cross-
  entropy, and probability computations.
- **Stable algorithms** (max-subtraction softmax, log-sum-exp, log probabilities) solve
  these problems with zero computational overhead.
- **Conditioning** determines how sensitive a computation is to input perturbations;
  ill-conditioned problems require special treatment (regularisation).
- **Vanishing and exploding gradients** are the chain-rule manifestation of numerical
  instability in deep networks, addressed by modern architectures (ReLU, ResNet, LSTM)
  and training techniques (gradient clipping, batch normalisation, proper initialisation).
- **Mixed-precision training** (FP16 compute, FP32 master weights) is the industry standard
  that balances speed and stability.

### Why This Matters for AI

Every training crash, every NaN in a loss log, every model that refuses to converge is
ultimately a **numerical stability problem**. Understanding the mathematics of finite
precision computation is what separates an AI practitioner who can diagnose and fix these
issues from one who is helplessly staring at NaN.

### Reflection Questions (not assessed)

1. You train a transformer model and the loss suddenly becomes NaN at epoch 50. List three
   possible numerical causes and how you would diagnose each.
2. Why does BF16 use 8 exponent bits instead of the 5 in standard FP16? What practical
   problem does this solve?
3. A colleague argues that FP64 should always be used to avoid numerical issues. Why is
   this impractical for modern AI?
4. How does batch normalisation help with both vanishing gradients AND ill-conditioning?

---

**End of Week 11 Lab — Well done!** 🎉

*If you found any errors or have suggestions, please raise them in the next seminar session.*
questions.explanation:
Conditioning measures how much output changes when input changes slightly. Ill-conditioned systems amplify errors.

questions.prompt:
### Challenge 1 — Mixed-Precision Training Simulation

Simulate the effect of training a linear regression model in FP32 vs FP16:
1. Generate data from $y = 3x + 2 + \varepsilon$.
2. Train with gradient descent in FP64, FP32, and FP16 for 500 epochs.
3. Plot the loss curves and final parameter estimates for each precision.
4. Identify at what point FP16 training stalls or diverges.

---
questions.code:
# Complete solution: Mixed-Precision Training Simulation
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

np.random.seed(7)
n = 200
x = np.linspace(-2, 2, n)
noise = np.random.normal(0, 0.25, size=n)
y = 3 * x + 2 + noise

def train(dtype, epochs=500, eta=0.05):
    x_d = x.astype(dtype)
    y_d = y.astype(dtype)
    w = dtype(0.0)
    b = dtype(0.0)
    losses = []
    params = []
    stalled_epoch = None

    for epoch in range(epochs):
        y_hat = w * x_d + b
        err = y_hat - y_d
        loss = np.mean((err.astype(np.float64)) ** 2)
        losses.append(loss)
        params.append((float(w), float(b)))

        dw = dtype(2.0) * np.mean(err * x_d).astype(dtype)
        db = dtype(2.0) * np.mean(err).astype(dtype)
        old_w, old_b = w, b
        w = dtype(w - dtype(eta) * dw)
        b = dtype(b - dtype(eta) * db)

        if stalled_epoch is None and float(w) == float(old_w) and float(b) == float(old_b):
            stalled_epoch = epoch

    return np.array(losses), params, float(w), float(b), stalled_epoch

results = {}
for dtype in [np.float64, np.float32, np.float16]:
    losses, params, w, b, stalled = train(dtype)
    results[dtype.__name__] = (losses, w, b, stalled)
    print(f"{dtype.__name__}: w={w:.6f}, b={b:.6f}, final loss={losses[-1]:.6f}, stalled={stalled}")

plt.figure(figsize=(9, 5))
for name, (losses, _, _, _) in results.items():
    plt.plot(losses, label=name)
plt.yscale("log")
plt.xlabel("Epoch")
plt.ylabel("MSE loss")
plt.title("Gradient descent under different floating-point precisions")
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.show()

print("\nInterpretation:")
print("FP64 and FP32 should converge smoothly to approximately w=3, b=2.")
print("FP16 may converge less accurately, stall, or show a noisier loss curve because updates are rounded more aggressively.")
questions.modelAnswer: This simulation compares the same gradient descent problem under FP64, FP32, and FP16. FP16 can stall because small updates may be below the precision available at the current parameter scale.

questions.prompt:
### Challenge 2 — Gradient Magnitude Through a Deep Network

Simulate gradient backpropagation through a 100-layer network with random weight matrices
of different initialisations:
1. Standard Normal $W \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$
2. Xavier initialisation $W \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1/n)$
3. He initialisation $W \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 2/n)$

For each, plot the gradient magnitude at each layer and identify which initialisation
maintains stable gradients.
questions.code:
# Complete solution: Gradient Magnitude Through a Deep Network
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

np.random.seed(11)
n = 64
layers = 100

def simulate(init_name):
    grad = np.random.normal(0, 1, size=(n, 1))
    norms = [np.linalg.norm(grad)]

    for _ in range(layers):
        if init_name == "standard":
            W = np.random.normal(0, 1, size=(n, n))
        elif init_name == "xavier":
            W = np.random.normal(0, np.sqrt(1 / n), size=(n, n))
        elif init_name == "he":
            W = np.random.normal(0, np.sqrt(2 / n), size=(n, n))
        else:
            raise ValueError(init_name)

        grad = W.T @ grad
        norms.append(np.linalg.norm(grad))

    return np.array(norms)

results = {
    "Standard N(0,1)": simulate("standard"),
    "Xavier N(0,1/n)": simulate("xavier"),
    "He N(0,2/n)": simulate("he"),
}

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
for label, norms in results.items():
    plt.plot(norms, label=label)
plt.yscale("log")
plt.xlabel("Backpropagated layer")
plt.ylabel("Gradient norm")
plt.title("Gradient magnitude through 100 layers")
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.show()

for label, norms in results.items():
    ratio = norms[-1] / norms[0]
    print(f"{label}: initial={norms[0]:.3e}, final={norms[-1]:.3e}, final/initial={ratio:.3e}")

print("\nInterpretation:")
print("Standard normal usually explodes because each layer multiplies the gradient by a large random matrix.")
print("Xavier keeps the scale closer to stable for linear/tanh-like activations.")
print("He has larger variance and is often suitable with ReLU activations, but can grow more than Xavier in a pure linear simulation.")
questions.modelAnswer: This answer backpropagates a random gradient through 100 linear layers and tracks the norm. Standard normal weights usually explode; Xavier is usually most stable for tanh/linear-style networks; He is designed for ReLU-style networks.

questions.prompt:
### Task 1: Vector Similarity with Dot Product

**Objective**: Understand how dot products measure similarity between vectors.

**Instructions**:
1. Create three document vectors (representing word counts):
   - `doc1 = [5, 2, 0, 8]` (represents counts of words: "AI", "data", "cooking", "learning")
   - `doc2 = [4, 3, 0, 7]`
   - `doc3 = [0, 0, 10, 1]`
2. Compute the dot product between:
   - doc1 and doc2
   - doc1 and doc3
3. Which document is more similar to doc1? Why?
4. Normalize the vectors (divide by their magnitude) and recompute dot products

**Expected outcome**: You should see that doc1 and doc2 are more similar (both about AI/learning), while doc3 is different (about cooking).

### Assessment Task 1: Feature Engineering and Linear Model (30 points)

**Scenario**: You're building a student performance predictor.

**Given Data**:
- 8 students with features: [study_hours_weekly, previous_exam_score, attendance_%, assignments_completed]
- Labels: final exam scores (0-100)

**Requirements**:

1. **Data Preparation (10 points)**:
   - Create the dataset matrix X (8×4) with realistic values
   - Create the label vector y (8 exam scores)
   - Normalize X using z-score normalization
   - Print statistics: mean, std, min, max for each feature

2. **Model Implementation (10 points)**:
   - Design a weight vector w (4 elements) and bias b
   - Implement the prediction: `predictions = X @ w + b`
   - Ensure predictions are in range [0, 100]

3. **Evaluation (10 points)**:
   - Compute Mean Absolute Error: $\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum|y_{\text{pred}} - y_{\text{true}}|$
   - Print predictions vs actual for each student
   - Identify which features have the largest impact (largest weight magnitudes)
   - Explain your weight choices
questions.code:
# Complete solution: Vector Similarity with Dot Product
import numpy as np

doc1 = np.array([5, 2, 0, 8])
doc2 = np.array([4, 3, 0, 7])
doc3 = np.array([0, 0, 10, 1])

dot_12 = np.dot(doc1, doc2)
dot_13 = np.dot(doc1, doc3)

print("Raw dot products:")
print("doc1 · doc2 =", dot_12)
print("doc1 · doc3 =", dot_13)
print("More similar to doc1:", "doc2" if dot_12 > dot_13 else "doc3")

def normalize(v):
    norm = np.linalg.norm(v)
    return v / norm if norm != 0 else v

doc1_n = normalize(doc1)
doc2_n = normalize(doc2)
doc3_n = normalize(doc3)

cos_12 = np.dot(doc1_n, doc2_n)
cos_13 = np.dot(doc1_n, doc3_n)

print("\nNormalized dot products / cosine similarities:")
print("cos(doc1, doc2) =", round(cos_12, 4))
print("cos(doc1, doc3) =", round(cos_13, 4))
print("\nInterpretation: doc1 and doc2 point in almost the same direction, so they represent similar content.")
questions.modelAnswer: Raw dot products show document 2 is more similar to document 1. After normalization, the dot product becomes cosine similarity and still shows doc2 is much closer in direction.
questions.explanation:
Vector similarity measures whether two vectors point in similar directions. Cosine similarity normalizes by vector length.

questions.prompt:
### Task 2: Build a Dataset Matrix

**Objective**: Practice creating dataset matrices from individual samples.

**Instructions**:
1. Create 5 student vectors with features: [hours_studied, previous_grade, attendance_%]
   - Student 1: [10, 75, 90]
   - Student 2: [15, 85, 95]
   - Student 3: [5, 60, 70]
   - Student 4: [12, 80, 88]
   - Student 5: [8, 70, 85]
2. Stack them into a dataset matrix X
3. Compute and print:
   - Mean of each feature
   - Standard deviation of each feature
   - The student with the most study hours (hint: use `np.argmax` on the first column)
4. Normalize the data by subtracting mean and dividing by std for each feature

**Expected outcome**: A 5×3 matrix with computed statistics and normalized version.

### Assessment Task 2: Image Classification Pipeline (30 points)

**Scenario**: Build a simple pipeline to classify small images.

**Requirements**:

1. **Data Generation (10 points)**:
   - Create 6 synthetic 8×8 "images" (3 of class 0, 3 of class 1)
   - Class 0: mostly bright pixels (values 200-255)
   - Class 1: mostly dark pixels (values 0-55)
   - Display all 6 images in a 2×3 grid

2. **Preprocessing (10 points)**:
   - Flatten each 8×8 image to a 64-dimensional vector
   - Stack into dataset matrix X (6×64)
   - Normalize pixels to [0, 1] range by dividing by 255

3. **Classification (10 points)**:
   - Create weight vector w (64 elements) and bias b
   - Compute scores: `scores = X @ w + b`
   - Classify: score > 0 → class 1, score ≤ 0 → class 0
   - Report accuracy
   - Explain: Why does the mean pixel value help classify these images?

**Hint**: Since bright images (class 0) have high pixel values and dark images (class 1) have low pixel values, the average pixel value is discriminative.
questions.code:
# Solution to Task 2: Build a Dataset Matrix

# Step 1: Create student vectors
student1 = np.array([10, 75, 90])
student2 = np.array([15, 85, 95])
student3 = np.array([5, 60, 70])
student4 = np.array([12, 80, 88])
student5 = np.array([8, 70, 85])

# Step 2: Stack into matrix
X = np.vstack([student1, student2, student3, student4, student5])

print("Student Dataset Matrix X:")
print("Columns: [hours_studied, previous_grade, attendance_%]")
print(X)
print(f"Shape: {X.shape}")
print()

# Step 3: Compute statistics
means = np.mean(X, axis=0)
stds = np.std(X, axis=0)

print("Feature Statistics:")
print(f"Mean: {means}")
print(f"Std:  {stds}")
print()

# Find student with most study hours
max_hours_idx = np.argmax(X[:, 0])
print(f"Student with most study hours: Student {max_hours_idx + 1} ({X[max_hours_idx, 0]} hours)")
print()

# Step 4: Normalize using z-score
X_normalized = (X - means) / stds

print("Normalized Dataset (z-score):")
print(X_normalized)
print()

# Verify normalization
print("Verification (normalized data should have mean≈0, std≈1):")
print(f"Mean: {np.mean(X_normalized, axis=0)}")
print(f"Std:  {np.std(X_normalized, axis=0)}")

print("\nKey Learning Points:")
print("- Each row is a complete sample (student)")
print("- Each column is a feature across all students")
print("- axis=0 operations work down columns (across samples)")
print("- Z-score normalization: (x - μ) / σ centers data at 0 with unit variance")

questions.prompt:
### Task 3: Matrix Multiplication Practice

**Objective**: Master matrix multiplication and dimension checking.

**Instructions**:
1. Create matrix A (3×4) with random values using `np.random.randn(3, 4)`
2. Create matrix B (4×2) with random values
3. Compute C = A @ B
4. Verify the shape of C
5. Try to compute B @ A. What happens? Why?
6. Create a matrix D (2×3) and compute E = C @ D
7. Print all shapes and verify the dimension rule: (m×n) @ (n×p) = (m×p)

**Expected outcome**: Understanding of matrix multiplication rules and dimension compatibility.

### Assessment Task 3: Multi-Output Regression (40 points)

**Scenario**: Predict multiple properties of houses simultaneously.

**Given**: Houses with features [size, bedrooms, bathrooms, age]

**Predict**: [price, days_to_sell, renovation_cost]

**Requirements**:

1. **Dataset Creation (10 points)**:
   - Create training data: 10 houses with 4 features each
   - Create target matrix Y (10×3) with realistic values for 3 outputs
   - Normalize both X and Y

2. **Model Implementation (15 points)**:
   - Implement: $Y = XW^T + B$ where:
     - X is 10×4 (samples × input features)
     - W is 3×4 (output features × input features)
     - B is 3×1 (bias for each output)
     - Y is 10×3 (samples × outputs)
   - Initialize W and B with random values
   - Compute predictions for all samples

3. **Analysis (15 points)**:
   - Compute MSE for each output: $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum(y_{\text{pred}} - y_{\text{true}})^2$
   - Create 2 new test samples and predict their outputs
   - Visualize predictions vs actual for one output (scatter plot)
   - **Written explanation**:
     - Why is matrix multiplication appropriate for multi-output prediction?
     - How do the dimensions of W determine the transformation?
     - What does each row of W represent?
questions.code:
# Solution to Task 3: Matrix Multiplication Practice

# Step 1 & 2: Create matrices
np.random.seed(42)  # For reproducibility
A = np.random.randn(3, 4)
B = np.random.randn(4, 2)

print("Matrix A (3×4):")
print(A)
print(f"Shape: {A.shape}")
print()

print("Matrix B (4×2):")
print(B)
print(f"Shape: {B.shape}")
print()

# Step 3 & 4: Compute C and verify shape
C = A @ B
print("Matrix C = A @ B:")
print(C)
print(f"Shape: {C.shape}")
print(f"Dimension rule: ({A.shape[0]}×{A.shape[1]}) @ ({B.shape[0]}×{B.shape[1]}) = ({C.shape[0]}×{C.shape[1]}) ✓")
print()

# Step 5: Try B @ A
print("Attempting B @ A:")
try:
    result = B @ A
    print("Success! Result shape:", result.shape)
except ValueError as e:
    print(f"Error: {e}")
    print(f"Explanation: B is {B.shape} and A is {A.shape}")
    print(f"For B @ A, we need: inner dimensions to match")
    print(f"B has {B.shape[1]} columns, A has {A.shape[0]} rows")
    print(f"{B.shape[1]} ≠ {A.shape[0]}, so multiplication is invalid!")
print()

# Step 6 & 7: Compute E and verify dimensions
D = np.random.randn(2, 3)
E = C @ D

print("Matrix D (2×3):")
print(D)
print(f"Shape: {D.shape}")
print()

print("Matrix E = C @ D:")
print(E)
print(f"Shape: {E.shape}")
print()

print("Complete Chain:")
print(f"A: {A.shape} → B: {B.shape} → C: {C.shape} → D: {D.shape} → E: {E.shape}")
print("\nDimension Rule: (m×n) @ (n×p) = (m×p)")
print("The inner dimensions (n) must match!")
print("The outer dimensions (m, p) determine the result shape.")

questions.prompt:
### Task 4: Implement a Simple Linear Transformation

**Objective**: Build the fundamental neural network operation: $\mathbf{y} = W\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}$

**Instructions**:
1. Create an input vector x with 5 features: [1.0, 2.5, -0.5, 3.0, 1.5]
2. Create a weight matrix W (3×5) that transforms 5 inputs to 3 outputs (use random values)
3. Create a bias vector b (3 elements) with values [0.5, -0.3, 0.1]
4. Compute y = W @ x + b
5. Print x, W, b, and y with their shapes
6. Verify: input dimension = 5, output dimension = 3

**Extension**: Apply this transformation to a batch of 10 samples (create X with shape 10×5)

**Expected outcome**: A working linear transformation, the building block of neural networks.
questions.code:
# Solution to Task 4: Linear Transformation

# Step 1: Create input vector
x = np.array([1.0, 2.5, -0.5, 3.0, 1.5])

print("Input Vector x:")
print(x)
print(f"Shape: {x.shape}")
print(f"Dimension: {len(x)}")
print()

# Step 2: Create weight matrix
np.random.seed(42)
W = np.random.randn(3, 5)  # 3 outputs, 5 inputs

print("Weight Matrix W (3×5):")
print(W)
print(f"Shape: {W.shape}")
print()

# Step 3: Create bias vector
b = np.array([0.5, -0.3, 0.1])

print("Bias Vector b:")
print(b)
print(f"Shape: {b.shape}")
print()

# Step 4: Compute transformation
y = W @ x + b

print("Output y = Wx + b:")
print(y)
print(f"Shape: {y.shape}")
print()

# Step 5 & 6: Verify dimensions
print("Dimension Verification:")
print(f"Input dimension: {len(x)} features")
print(f"Weight matrix: {W.shape[0]} outputs × {W.shape[1]} inputs")
print(f"Output dimension: {len(y)} values")
print(f"Check: {W.shape[0]} outputs = {len(y)} ✓")
print()

# Extension: Batch processing
print("=" * 60)
print("EXTENSION: Batch Processing")
print("=" * 60)
print()

# Create batch of 10 samples
X_batch = np.random.randn(10, 5)

print("Input Batch X (10 samples × 5 features):")
print(X_batch)
print(f"Shape: {X_batch.shape}")
print()

# Process all samples at once
Y_batch = X_batch @ W.T + b  # Note: W.T transposes W

print("Output Batch Y (10 samples × 3 outputs):")
print(Y_batch)
print(f"Shape: {Y_batch.shape}")
print()

print("Batch Processing Advantages:")
print("1. Process all samples in one operation (efficient)")
print("2. Leverages optimized matrix multiplication")
print("3. Essential for GPU acceleration in deep learning")
print("4. Same weights applied to all samples (parameter sharing)")

questions.prompt:
### Task 5: Image Flattening and Reconstruction

**Objective**: Understand how images are converted to vectors and back.

**Instructions**:
1. Create a 10×10 "image" with random integers from 0 to 255
2. Display it using matplotlib (with `cmap='gray'`)
3. Flatten it to a 1D vector
4. Print the shape before and after flattening
5. Reshape the flattened vector back to 10×10
6. Display the reconstructed image
7. Verify they are identical using `np.array_equal()`

**Expected outcome**: Understanding that flattening is reversible and how images become vectors.
questions.code:
# Solution to Task 5: Image Flattening

# Step 1: Create image
np.random.seed(42)
image = np.random.randint(0, 256, size=(10, 10))

print("Original Image (10×10):")
print(image)
print(f"Shape: {image.shape}")
print(f"Data type: {image.dtype}")
print()

# Step 2: Display original image
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 5))

axes[0].imshow(image, cmap='gray', vmin=0, vmax=255)
axes[0].set_title('Original Image (10×10)')
axes[0].axis('off')

# Step 3: Flatten
flattened = image.flatten()
print("Flattened Vector:")
print(flattened)
print(f"Shape: {flattened.shape}")
print(f"Length: {len(flattened)} = 10 × 10 = 100")
print()

# Step 4: Print shapes
print("Shape Transformation:")
print(f"Before flattening: {image.shape} (2D matrix)")
print(f"After flattening:  {flattened.shape} (1D vector)")
print(f"Total elements: {image.size} (unchanged)")
print()

# Step 5: Reshape back
reconstructed = flattened.reshape(10, 10)
print("Reconstructed Image (10×10):")
print(reconstructed)
print(f"Shape: {reconstructed.shape}")
print()

# Step 6: Display reconstructed image
axes[1].imshow(reconstructed, cmap='gray', vmin=0, vmax=255)
axes[1].set_title('Reconstructed Image (10×10)')
axes[1].axis('off')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Step 7: Verify they're identical
are_identical = np.array_equal(image, reconstructed)
print(f"\nAre original and reconstructed identical? {are_identical}")

if are_identical:
    print("✓ Flattening is reversible!")
else:
    print("✗ Something went wrong")

print("\nKey Concepts:")
print("1. Flattening converts 2D image to 1D vector (required for many ML models)")
print("2. Reshaping is reversible—no data is lost")
print("3. Total number of elements stays the same: rows × cols = vector length")
print("4. Real example: MNIST digits (28×28) → 784-dimensional vectors")
questions.modelAnswer:
### Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them

#### 1. **Dimension Mismatches in Matrix Multiplication**

**Mistake**: Trying to multiply incompatible matrices
```python
A = np.array([[1, 2], [3, 4]])  # 2×2
B = np.array([[1], [2], [3]])   # 3×1
C = A @ B  # Error! 2 ≠ 3
```

**Solution**: Always check shapes before multiplying
```python
print(f"A: {A.shape}, B: {B.shape}")
# Can we multiply? A's columns must equal B's rows
if A.shape[1] == B.shape[0]:
    C = A @ B
```

#### 2. **Broadcasting Confusion**

**Mistake**: Expecting element-wise when you get broadcasting
```python
A = np.array([[1, 2], [3, 4]])
b = np.array([1, 2])
result = A + b  # What happens?
```

**What happens**: NumPy broadcasts b across all rows
```python
# Result:
# [[1+1, 2+2],  = [[2, 4],
#  [3+1, 4+2]]  =  [4, 6]]
```

**Solution**: Understand broadcasting rules or reshape explicitly

#### 3. **Forgetting to Transpose**

**Mistake**: Wrong orientation in batch processing
```python
X = np.random.randn(100, 10)  # 100 samples, 10 features
W = np.random.randn(5, 10)     # 5 outputs, 10 inputs
Y = X @ W  # Error! 10 ≠ 5
```

**Solution**: Transpose W
```python
Y = X @ W.T  # Now: (100×10) @ (10×5) = 100×5 ✓
```

#### 4. **Axis Confusion**

**Mistake**: Using wrong axis for operations
```python
X = np.array([[1, 2, 3],
              [4, 5, 6]])
mean = np.mean(X, axis=1)  # Mean of each row? or column?
```

**Solution**: Remember:
- `axis=0`: operate along rows (→ column results)
- `axis=1`: operate along columns (→ row results)

```python
mean_per_feature = np.mean(X, axis=0)  # [2.5, 3.5, 4.5]
mean_per_sample = np.mean(X, axis=1)   # [2.0, 5.0]
```

questions.prompt:
### Challenge 1: Cosine Similarity

The **cosine similarity** between two vectors measures their directional similarity:

$$\text{cosine\_similarity}(\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}) = \frac{\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v}}{\|\mathbf{u}\| \|\mathbf{v}\|}$$

Range: [-1, 1] where 1 = identical direction, 0 = perpendicular, -1 = opposite

**Task**:
1. Implement a function `cosine_similarity(u, v)` using NumPy
2. Test it on the document vectors from Task 1
3. Compare cosine similarity with raw dot products—what's different?
4. Create two perpendicular vectors and verify their cosine similarity ≈ 0
questions.code:
# Complete solution: Cosine Similarity
import numpy as np

def cosine_similarity(u, v):
    u = np.asarray(u, dtype=float)
    v = np.asarray(v, dtype=float)
    denom = np.linalg.norm(u) * np.linalg.norm(v)
    if denom == 0:
        return 0.0
    return float(np.dot(u, v) / denom)

doc1 = np.array([5, 2, 0, 8])
doc2 = np.array([4, 3, 0, 7])
doc3 = np.array([0, 0, 10, 1])

print("Cosine similarities:")
print("doc1 vs doc2:", cosine_similarity(doc1, doc2))
print("doc1 vs doc3:", cosine_similarity(doc1, doc3))

u = np.array([1, 0])
v = np.array([0, 1])
print("\nPerpendicular vectors:")
print("cos([1,0], [0,1]) =", cosine_similarity(u, v))

print("\nRaw dot products depend on vector length; cosine similarity focuses on direction.")
questions.modelAnswer: Cosine similarity measures direction rather than raw size. It is better than raw dot product when vector length should not dominate the comparison.
questions.explanation:
Vector similarity measures whether two vectors point in similar directions. Cosine similarity normalizes by vector length.

questions.prompt:
### Challenge 2: Two-Layer Neural Network

**Task**: Implement a two-layer transformation:

$$\mathbf{h} = W_1\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}_1$$
$$\mathbf{y} = W_2\mathbf{h} + \mathbf{b}_2$$

Where:
- Input: 4 features
- Hidden layer: 6 neurons
- Output: 2 values

1. Create appropriate weight matrices and bias vectors (with correct dimensions)
2. Process a single input vector
3. Process a batch of 5 samples
4. Add a ReLU activation between layers: `h = np.maximum(0, h)` (sets negative values to 0)
5. Visualize how the data transforms at each layer
questions.code:
# Complete solution: Two-Layer Neural Network
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

np.random.seed(42)

input_dim = 4
hidden_dim = 6
output_dim = 2

W1 = np.random.normal(0, 0.5, size=(hidden_dim, input_dim))
b1 = np.zeros(hidden_dim)
W2 = np.random.normal(0, 0.5, size=(output_dim, hidden_dim))
b2 = np.zeros(output_dim)

def relu(z):
    return np.maximum(0, z)

def forward_single(x):
    h_linear = W1 @ x + b1
    h = relu(h_linear)
    y = W2 @ h + b2
    return h_linear, h, y

x = np.array([2.0, 0.7, 0.9, 1.0])
h_linear, h, y = forward_single(x)
print("Single sample:")
print("h before ReLU:", h_linear)
print("h after ReLU:", h)
print("output y:", y)

X_batch = np.random.normal(0, 1, size=(5, input_dim))
H_linear = X_batch @ W1.T + b1
H = relu(H_linear)
Y = H @ W2.T + b2

print("\nBatch shapes:")
print("X_batch:", X_batch.shape)
print("H:", H.shape)
print("Y:", Y.shape)

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 4))
plt.subplot(1, 2, 1)
plt.imshow(H_linear, aspect="auto", cmap="coolwarm")
plt.title("Hidden pre-activation")
plt.colorbar()

plt.subplot(1, 2, 2)
plt.imshow(H, aspect="auto", cmap="viridis")
plt.title("Hidden after ReLU")
plt.colorbar()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
questions.modelAnswer: The key is dimensional consistency: W1 maps 4 input features to 6 hidden values, and W2 maps 6 hidden values to 2 output values. For batches, use X @ W.T + b.

questions.prompt:
### Challenge 3: Data Normalization Techniques

**Task**: Implement and compare three normalization methods:

1. **Min-Max Scaling**: Scale to [0, 1]
   $$x_{\text{scaled}} = \frac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$$

2. **Z-Score Normalization**: Mean 0, std 1
   $$x_{\text{normalized}} = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

3. **L2 Normalization**: Scale to unit length
   $$x_{\text{normalized}} = \frac{x}{\|x\|}$$

Apply each to the student dataset from Task 2 and visualize the differences.
questions.code:
# Complete solution: Data Normalization Techniques
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

X = np.array([
    [5, 72, 85, 8],
    [8, 88, 92, 10],
    [2, 55, 60, 5],
    [10, 95, 98, 12],
    [6, 78, 80, 9],
    [3, 62, 70, 6],
    [9, 90, 95, 11],
    [4, 68, 75, 7],
], dtype=float)

feature_names = ["study_hours", "previous_score", "attendance", "assignments"]

min_vals = X.min(axis=0)
max_vals = X.max(axis=0)
X_minmax = (X - min_vals) / (max_vals - min_vals)

means = X.mean(axis=0)
stds = X.std(axis=0)
X_zscore = (X - means) / stds

row_norms = np.linalg.norm(X, axis=1, keepdims=True)
X_l2 = X / row_norms

print("Original feature means:", X.mean(axis=0))
print("Min-max range per feature:", X_minmax.min(axis=0), X_minmax.max(axis=0))
print("Z-score means:", np.round(X_zscore.mean(axis=0), 6))
print("Z-score stds:", np.round(X_zscore.std(axis=0), 6))
print("L2 row norms:", np.round(np.linalg.norm(X_l2, axis=1), 6))

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(14, 4))
datasets = [X, X_minmax, X_zscore, X_l2]
titles = ["Original", "Min-Max", "Z-Score", "L2 row norm"]

for ax, data, title in zip(axes, datasets, titles):
    im = ax.imshow(data, aspect="auto", cmap="viridis")
    ax.set_title(title)
    ax.set_xticks(range(len(feature_names)))
    ax.set_xticklabels(feature_names, rotation=45, ha="right")
    ax.set_ylabel("Student")
    fig.colorbar(im, ax=ax, fraction=0.046)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
questions.modelAnswer: Min-max scaling changes range, z-score standardization changes mean/std, and L2 normalization changes each row length to 1. They answer different preprocessing needs.
